explanation: `s.substring(0, 1)` extracts only the first character of each String, and `System.out.print` (not `println`) appends them without spaces or newlines, so all three elements produce "ABC" consecutively. A would result if the full name were printed; B adds spaces the code never produces; D implies the loop exited after only the first element.

stem: A class `Item` has an instance method `getValue()` that returns an `int`. An `ArrayList<Item>` named `inventory` has been declared and populated. Which of the following correctly computes the sum of all values in `inventory`?

options.label: A
B
C
D

explanation: Option A correctly calls `getValue()` on each retrieved `Item` element `it`. Option B calls `getValue()` on the `ArrayList` object itself, which has no such method. Option C uses `<= inventory.size()` so when `i` reaches `inventory.size()`, `inventory.get(i)` throws an `IndexOutOfBoundsException`. Option D attempts to invoke `getValue()` as a static method using the class name, which is invalid for an instance method.

When `i = 1`, `list.get(1)` is `2` (even), so `list.remove(1)` executes and the second `2` shifts from index 2 to index 1. `i` then increments to 2, where `list.get(2)` is now `3`, so the shifted second `2` is never examined. The final list is `[1, 2, 3]`. Option A is what a correct removal loop would produce; C results if no elements are removed; D is wrong because `i < list.size()` dynamically adapts as the list shrinks, preventing out-of-bounds access.

stem: An `ArrayList<Integer>` named `data` contains five elements. Which of the following loop headers will cause an `IndexOutOfBoundsException` at runtime when `data.get(i)` is called inside the loop body?
explanation: Valid indices for a 5-element ArrayList are 0 through 4. Option C uses `i <= data.size()` (i.e., `i <= 5`), so when `i` reaches 5, `data.get(5)` throws `IndexOutOfBoundsException`. Options A and B are equivalent—both stop at the last valid index 4 (`data.size() - 1`). Option D correctly traverses in reverse from index 4 down to 0 without ever going out of bounds.

The loop runs for `i` = 0, 1, 2, 3 (stopping when `i < 4`). Comparing adjacent pairs: 3 > 7? No; 7 > 2? Yes (count = 1); 2 > 8? No; 8 > 1? Yes (count = 2). The bound `i < vals.size() - 1` ensures `vals.get(i + 1)` is always a valid access. Option A reflects finding only one descending pair; C overcounts; D is wrong because the loop bound prevents any out-of-bounds index.

stem: Which of the following most accurately describes when a `ConcurrentModificationException` is thrown during ArrayList traversal?

options.label: A
options.text: An element is accessed at an index equal to `list.size()` using `list.get()`

options.label: B
options.text: A `null` element is retrieved from the ArrayList and a method is called on it

options.label: C
options.text: An element is added to or removed from the ArrayList while an enhanced for loop is iterating over it

options.label: D
options.text: An element is added to or removed from the ArrayList while a standard indexed for loop is iterating over it

explanation: Java's enhanced for loop uses an internal iterator that detects structural changes (additions or removals that alter the list's size) and immediately throws `ConcurrentModificationException`. Option A causes `IndexOutOfBoundsException`; Option B causes `NullPointerException`; Option D is incorrect—structural modifications inside a standard indexed for loop do not throw this exception, though they may cause elements to be inadvertently skipped.
Only values divisible by 6 are changed, so 6 becomes 2 and 12 becomes 4. The other options either change all values, change none, or keep only the changed values.
After `at` is removed, `be` shifts to index 0 and is skipped when `i` increments to 1. `do` is later removed, leaving `[be, cat, egg]`.

options.label: C
D

explanation: Option A correctly visits every element and checks its first one-character substring. B checks only the first element repeatedly, C skips index 0, and D compares the whole string to `"a"` and skips the last element.
`"stars"` is the first string whose length is greater than 4, and it is at index 2. The other options reflect the position of shorter strings or missing the match.

stem: Which statement about traversing an `ArrayList<Integer>` is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: An indexed `for` loop can access both an element and its index.

options.label: B
options.text: An enhanced `for` loop gives direct access to each element's index.

options.label: C
options.text: `list.length` gives the number of elements in an `ArrayList`.

options.label: D
options.text: `list[i]` is used to access the element at index `i`.

explanation: An indexed loop uses `i`, so it can use both `i` and `list.get(i)`. Enhanced loops do not provide the index, and ArrayLists use `size()` and `get(i)`, not `length` or bracket access.
The loop adds only the even values, 4 and 10, for a sum of 14. Assigning `n = 0` changes only the loop variable, not the elements in `vals`.
Only the original even values at indices 0 and 2 are printed. Odd values are changed with set, but they are not printed during those iterations.

`blue` and `gold` have length 4, so they are not counted. Only `green` and `blue`? Actually `blue` is not greater than 4, so the counted words are only `green`; wait, this means the correct value is 1.

options.label: A
B
D

explanation: An ArrayList is traversed with indices from 0 through size() - 1, using get(i). Choice A goes one index too far, C treats each value as an index, and D uses array length syntax.
When a negative value is removed, the next value shifts into the same index, so the code correctly does not increment `i` in that case. All negative values are removed.

stem: A `Book` class has a method `public int getPages()`. Given `ArrayList<Book> shelf`, which code segment correctly counts how many books have more than 300 pages?

options.label: A
B
C
D

explanation: The enhanced for loop variable should be a `Book`, and the method is called on each `Book`. The other choices use the wrong loop variable type, call the method on the list, or use incorrect index bounds.
The traversal starts at index 3 and moves backward. The even indices encountered are 2 and then 0, so the printed values are `C` followed by `A`.

After removing `"a"`, `"bb"` shifts to index 0 and the loop moves to index 1, so `"c"` is then removed. The remaining elements are `"bb"` and `"dd"`; the other options reflect skipped or extra removals.

stem: Assume `names` is an `ArrayList<String>` containing only nonempty strings. Which of the following code segments correctly counts the number of strings in `names` that begin with `"A"`?

options.label: A
B
C
D

explanation: Option A traverses each element and uses `.equals` to compare string contents. Option B goes out of bounds, option C calls `substring` on the list instead of an element, and option D uses reference equality for strings.
The loop traverses backward, removing `8` and `4` while adding `6` and `2` to `total`. The final list is `[2, 6]`, and the sum is `8`.

options.label: A
options.text: It returns `false` after checking only the first word if that word is not longer than 6 characters.

options.label: C
options.text: It cannot use an enhanced `for` loop to examine strings in an `ArrayList`.

options.label: D
options.text: It should compare `w` to `6` directly instead of calling `length()`.

explanation: The `else` branch causes the method to return before checking the remaining elements. The other choices misuse array syntax, incorrectly reject enhanced traversal, or compare incompatible values.

stem: Which of the following code segments correctly removes all negative values from `values`, an `ArrayList<Integer>`?

options.label: A
C
D

explanation: Traversing backward prevents removals from shifting unvisited elements into skipped positions. Option B changes the list during enhanced traversal, option C can skip consecutive negatives, and option D can access an invalid index.
For two-character strings, the code appends the substring starting at index 1; for one-character strings, it appends the whole string. This produces `XBYD`.
When `4` is removed at index 1, `8` shifts into index 1, but `k` is then incremented to 2, so `8` is skipped. The remaining values printed are `2 8 10 `.
